read_calcmethod: SMD (Hedges' g) based on change score means and change score sds
justification: Cochrane Handbook recommendations ("conservative correlation of 0.5 was assumed to minimize the likelihood of overstating the intervention effect"), Lakens 2013 for SMD with Hedges' g
imbalance: half of the studies are non-randomized. The RoB checklist (Suppl 3) is not really suitable as its items are for RCTs. The RoB results are therefore questionable.
read_comments: The authors present the imputation of the pre-post correlation as a limitation. Interestingly, the methods section seem to be copy-pasted and included a wording not relevant to the study (stretching vs. control).
recalc_study: Almquist 2022 (Group 2) (41 of 68 in Sup Fig 1, but there might be some double-counting in the forest plot)
recalc_calcmethod: This one is tricky. From how I understand it the authors did in fact not perform any group comparison, but only calculated within changes for each group. They used SMD with Hedges' g correction for this (I could reproduce this with the number they give), but with treating the pre- and post-measures as independent. Thus not considering the pre-post correlation at all despite stating.
recalc_comments:
I also couldn't reproduce their data extraction. The numbers reported in the MA do neither exactly match the data from the plot or table of the original publication (which differ, by the way). So there may be several issues here. 

The main take-away is that the meta-analysis did not really use a group comparison at all despite stating this. Maybe Tab 2 indicates a potential between group comparison, but this rather looks like a comparison of different overall within-groups effects. Maybe the multi-level MA offers any additional information, but I can't find anything more on it. 

So I could reproduce the ES size measure, but not with the method stated. Also I could not reproduce the data extraction.

read_calcmethod: SMD of change score means devided by pooled change score sds. Unclear if Hedges' correction. Indirect link to Borenstein & Hedges for the variance estimator, which would mean that Hedges' correction applies (though their reference makes a mistake in copying the formula).
justification: commonly used, "balancing the risk of over- or underestimating the variance." Reference to Rosenthal
imbalance: only randomized or crossover studies included
recalc_study: Franchi et al (14 of 35 in Fig 2, though there is potentially double-counting of studies)
recalc_calcmethod: SMD of change scores standardized by the change score standard deviation, without Hedges' g correction. Variance calculation unclear
recalc_comments:
I had to try to recalculate three studies: The first and second study presented percentage differences with a measure of variability, so that no correlation imputation was needed. For the third study I could confirm the imputation procedure. In general I could reproduce the effect sizes (when not using Hedges' correct, the correction was not written in the manuscript but may have been implied by the references). I could not reproduce the CIs, maybe in part because I had to extract these values from a low quality figure. 

Also, for the first study I tried to recalculate, the MA authors mistook SEM for SD, making the effect estimate incorrect. In this case the CIs were also definetly off.

read_calcmethod: I guess post-score based SMD (Cohen's d), but I am not exactly sure because only studies with pre and post-measures were included.
justification: At some point Cochrane is referenced for the effect size measures selected, but no explicit justification.
imbalance: included RCTs and CTs, analyzed separately, ROBINS-I and RoB2 bias assessment.
recalc_study: Fu (2013), from Figure S1a (Suppl)
recalc_calcmethod: Cohen's d SMD based on change scores (standardized by either average pre and post standard deviation or by change score standard deviation when imputing r = 0.5).
recalc_imputemethod: maybe r=0.5 or average pre and post sd instead
recalc_comments: I had to use a second study to work out the effect size calculation procedure. It then worked for both. The MA neither mentioned any correlation imputation nor standard deviation combination procedure, and they provide comparable results, so I am unclear which of both was used. In general the article lacks any concrete description of the effect size calculation procedure.

read_calcmethod: SMD of change scores standardized by pooled pre sd (this is essentially the ppc2 approach by Morris 2008)
read_imputemethod: unclear (although not needed for standardization, but for variance estimate)
imbalance: randomized and non-randomized trials included, pedro scale rating
recalc_study: Hayes & Walker (2007) [1] (87 out of 106 from S6 Fig 1)
recalc_calcmethod: ppc2 of Morris (change score based SMD with pooled pre sd for standardization). Matches ES and almost matches CI.
recalc_comments: The authors provided the full extracted data with was extremely helpful for reanalyzing. I had to extract the original study data from a plot but received values similar to the MA authors. The MA authors made a mistake in data extraction for the sample size of the control group. The correlation imputation had to be guessed, but worked well (also in the raw data the authors commented that they used r=0.5 for calculating change score sd, which they did not use for their analysis).

read_calcmethod: SMD based on change scores standardized by pooled pre sd (Morris ppc2).
justification: reference to Morris for the effect size. reference to Rosenthal (1986) for the correlation imputation ("standard value of 0.7")
imbalance: only randomized controlled trials included
read_comments: The authors provide the formula for their ES and its variance.
recalc_study: Martínez Guardado (2020) bizeps curl (16 out of 31 from Fig 2)
recalc_calcmethod: SMD based on change scores standardized by pooled pre sd (Morris ppc2).
recalc_comments: recalculation perfectly worked with the first try

read_calcmethod: Hedges g with correction factor c= (1-3/4(n-1) using raw score standardisation (according to Becker & Morris)
read_imputemethod: p calculation as reported in the Cochrane handbook alternatively:  imputation of p=0.7

recalc_study: Simao, R. (Reference in forest plot wrong. 76 instead of 77), no. 20 in Figure 4
recalc_calcmethod: was able to recalculate the Effect size with the given equation (4) by Favro which is in line with Morris 2008. I was not able to recalculate CI / variance as the change SD was not given and r=0.7 led to different CI.

read_calcmethod: MD
justification:
"given that the included studies were exclusively randomized, and any observed
differences at postintervention were theoretically attributed to the intervention itself."

As a surrogate if no post-scores are available, the MA uses change score values. This is extensively justified by an explanation, a reference to the Cochrane handbook, and to earlier articles using the combined approach. Generally, the justification of methods is very detailed.

read_calcmethod: MD

Interestingly while searching for any study effect sizes for recalculation, I found that in the protocol for preregistration, the authors did explicitly state their imputation method (using r = 0.5). But since this has been written before the study and not after it (and is also difficult to find), I do not take this as an extraction result.
recalc_study: none
recalc_calcmethod: unclear

read_calcmethod: MD
imbalance: only RCTs or cluster-RCTs included. ROB-2 for bias assessment
read_comments: sufficient reporting for this type of ES, but no justification given
recalc_study: Wolfenden et al. 2019 (45 out of 45 from Fig 2)
recalc_calcmethod: MD of post score (ith a variance estimator assuming homogeneous variances)
recalc_comments: The target variable had to be recalculated because it was given in absolute instead of relative units, but this worked well given that the forest plot shows individual group means and sds. When using the standard MD (not assuming homogeneous variances), one CI bound was narrowly off (by 0.01%). Assuming homogeneous variances matched the result, but I am unsure if the authors did indeed not use the RevMan software default here. Also for these numbers, it is practically irrelevant.

read_calcmethod: SMD or MD depending on whether different scales were used
imbalance: only RCTs included, TESTEX for bias assessment

recalc_study: Arthur et al. 2007 (9 out of 16 in Fig 2a, because Yamasaki et al. 1985 (8 out of 16) could not be retrieved and also is in Japanese)
recalc_calcmethod: SMD (Hedges' g) based on change scores and their standard deviations. Probably also assuming homogeneous variance and using a particular variance estimator (both RevMan defaults), but not possible to prove because different selections yield similar result.
recalc_comments:
I could recalculate the ES and its CI, but only when ignoring different issues I regard as errors. By luck, this study was a special case indicated in the MA text, because it did not present post score sds. Regardless of this I could do the recalculation and found the following issues:
1. The percentage changes are taking as the change scores. This is problematic, because for their sds, the absolute scale is used
2. I think one percentage was incorrectly extracted (18% instead of 19%)
3. The group assignment of percentage changes is incorrect, so the effect is in the wrong direction.

The ES is reproducible when considering these errors, but it is wrong in its magnitude and direction (only its precision is correct).

The text says: "Effect sizes (ES) were calculated based on the change score (mean difference, MD) between experimental and control groups in post-measures to demonstrate the impact of exercise." I don't know if this means the used change scores or used post-measures. 

On another note, recalculation may be difficult as I do not see an individual study forest plot.

recalc_comments:
okay, difficult. First, because the MA forest plot does not report numbers, so individual study effects have to be extracted from the plot. Second, because all original research I reviewer also did not report numbers, but only figures, so again data has to be extracted from plots.

Overall I tried 3 studies (and skipped another). (1) was Gorman & Ferron, I could not come anywhere close to the ES here. Also there were inconsistencies in the original work. I skipped the next one because the MA states it used two outcomes from it without telling how they were combined. (2) was Smeeton where may have gotten somewhere in the range of the target ES, but couldn't continue because the original data figure was missing clear information. (3) Murgia, I could get near the target ES using the methods described in the MA text, so given the inaccuracies of two plot extraction processes here, this could maybe be considered a successful reproduction. But I have no idea how the ES CI was calculated.

read_calcmethod: MD of change scores (and change score sds)

imbalance: only RCTs includede, ROB-2 for bias assessment

recalc_study: Spillane et al. 2009 (12 out of 12 from Fig 2)
recalc_calcmethod: MD (not assuming homoscedasticity)
recalc_imputemethod: 0.975 (in this instance, so exactly as the authors described it)
recalc_comments: I could fully reproduce the ES, but only when I multiplied the control group sample size, mean, and sd by 1/2. I think this was done because there are two intervention group for this particular study (though not in the same forest plot) and the MA authors used this to account for potential double-counting). But then one should halve the sample size and definitely not also the mean and sd. This can (and in this instance does) highly bias ES towards exaggerating treatment effects. I found this out by looking at the data sheet the MA authors provided on the OSF, so providing data and code here proved to be extremely helpful. (On another note, the reference number was incorrect in the forest plot)

The MA states that in case they were "similar", r = 0.5 was chosen for the MA, and from Tab 1 it seems that in fact r = 0.5 was always used.

When taking these values, I could reproduce the ES. (I also checked for another study that values were correctly extracted). I did not check the correlation imputation, as it was not necessary for the studies I looked at. The MA states it was actually only necessary for one study, but its Table 1 suggests that imputation was done for 3 studies. I looked at them but the data extraction seems non-trivial, so I did not formally test the imputation procedure. Also, the reference number in the forest plot was incorrect.

I could fully reproduce the ES and CI but also after some digging and contacting the author of MedCalc. The software uses the vartype=2 estimator and t-distribution based CIs, which is both rather uncommon and undocumented in the software and also in this article.
Also, I tried to reproduce the imputation method, but only the third study tried (Alarcon-Gomez) did not report change score sd and thus needed imputation. The reproduced imputation (r=0) was noteworthy!

this is an umbrella review, but has a dedicated MA of RCTs. The effect size calculation method is very confusing: "The absolute change in mean difference and standard deviation
of the outcome value from postintervention between groups (interval training versus MICT/CON) in each study was calculated". This could be both change-score based or post-score based. Maybe also change score with post score sd standardization?

read_comments:
This study does not formally have a group comparison. Analyses are performed within groups, including studies that had no comparison group. So the research design may not fully correspond to what we are looking for. There are two possible ways to deal with this: 
(1) exclude the study because it does not exactly match the design we are looking for
(2) include the study because the abstract suggests a design that does match our criteria and only the analysis method was not corresponding and should be treated as a special case.

read_calcmethod: SMD (but unclear based on what, maybe post score)

recalc_calcmethod: unclear
recalc_comments: Frankly, I have no idea how the ES were calculated. The confidence intervals seem way to small. I tried change-score and post score based methods for three examples. Post-score seems more promising to me, but again I could not recalculate the ES.

read_calcmethod: SMD (Hedges' g), more details are available in Sup. 9. Unclear if change or post-score based, but definitely a form of within group analysis (due to the crossover design). Imputation is based on average correlations for the same outcome, or if not available r = 0.5.
justification: imputation based on previous published (and unpublished) data, link to Cochrane handbook for ES calculation
imbalance: only RCTs included, ROB-2 for bias assessment
read_comments:
Suppl S9 is very detailed. The procedures follow the standard for crossover (so within group analysis), but some formula are confusing. For example the hedges ES is actually not corrected, but simple Cohen's d. 
The analyzed studies are cross-over trial, which influences the choice of ES method.
The study has published raw data and code, which should help during the recalculation.
recalc_study: Carter et al. 2018 (1 out of 7 from Fig S1), condition 2x8min
recalc_calcmethod: SMD based on within-group comparisons (with [possibly incorrect] bias corrected variance but not ES). Correlation imputation based on average values for all included studies (though study level results were available).
recalc_comments:
Using the published data and code I could recalculate the ES with CI (otherwise it would be challenging even with the detailed Sup S9). Some things were noteworthy:
1. The MA uses imputed mean correlations for calculating the study level effect size, even if the study has correlation data available
2. within-condition pre-post correlations are computed, but in the analysis they are treated as between-condition post correlations.
3. There was a small data extraction error for the SD_change, extracting 1.6 instead of 1.8. This changed the imputed correlation, but had no impact on the result, because:
4. There was an error in the Excel reference for the recalculated study, using the mean correlation for the wrong outcome measure for the variance calculation (for 'Cerebrovascular conductance' instead of 'Cerebral blood flow velocity', which is 0.69 instead of 0.72)
5. For the ES point estimate, the data sheet and MA report to use Hedges' g, but it is incorrectly calculated as a simple SMD without correction using the pooled sd as standardizer. So in fact this is Cohen's d and for equal n the exact same value as the ES the data sheet reports as uncorrected (which uses average sd instead of pooled sd)
6. Also the correction procedure for the ES variance does not match the ES variance calculation. The variance calculation assumes a repeated measure design (so df=n-1), but the correction formula uses df=n1+n2-2).

So there are a lot of small issues going on here. And most of them could only be identified because the raw data and code was available.

I tried to recalculate the study level effect size by using the correct (mean) study correlation and fully using the appropriate Hedges' correction (so in fact trying to fix the points (1,3,5,6) above:
old ES: 0.32 [-0.08, 0.71]
new ES: 0.29 [-0.10, 0.70]

So in this particular single-study case, it does not make a substantial difference.

The first I could retrieve had raw data available, from which I could calculate the group means in the forest plot, but not the group sds. So there may be a data extraction error. For the second study I could receive I had another issue, as I could match the mean and sd of the intervention but not the control group (so maybe another extraction error). For the third study everything worked, when imputing a pre-post correlation of r = 0.5.

read_calcmethod: SMD of within-group SMD, both with Hedges' correction and the same variance estimator
read_imputemethod: unclear, based on available data (but unclear what if not enough data is reported)
justification: Lakens 2013 for calculating the within group ES and using the Hedges' correction, Kebede et al. 2019 for calculating the correlation from reported sds, Madeyski & Kitchenham 2018 for the between group SMD, Goulet-Pelletier & Cousineau (2018) for the variance estimator
imbalance: only crossover trials included, self-developed checklist for bias-assessment
read_comments: The procedure is well-explained, well-referenced, and the appendix provides all formula used. I am unsure whether the used approach is mathematically correct, because (1) the variance estimator is for independent groups but also used for the within group ES here (2) there is an error (typo?) in the formula for calculating the pre-post correlation (3) the practice of standardizing a difference of standardized means may yield results that are different to interpret (4) The cross-over nature of the trials is not fully taken into account (e.g., not using the between condition correlation).
recalc_study: Jones et al. 2008 (9 out of 11 from Fig 2A)
recalc_calcmethod: SMD based on within-group SMD (both with Hedges' correction and both with an independent group variance estimator).
recalc_comments:
I could finally perfectly recalculate a given effect size, but only when following the mistakes from the MA analysis: 
1. the formula for calculating the pre-post correlation is not just incorrectly applied but also contains an error, with the result that a correlation of r = 0.5 is always imputed
2. the variance formula (which is inappropriate for the within group design) also has an error in the calculation of the degrees of freedom, as has the Hedges' correction factor
3. The standardization procedure of the between group ES standardizes already standardized SMDs by raw score SDs, which will almost always leads to extremely small ES (but large CIs)
4. The between group ES does not take the crossover design into account but treats the am and pm groups as independent (loss of power)

recalc_comments: I could perfectly recalculate the ES and CI for 2 out of 3 cases when using a uncorrected SMD with raw (post!) score standardization. This means that the references paper Morris 2008 was not followed, since it recommends the Hedges' correction and pre-score sd standardization. It also means that the assumed correlation of r = 0.7 has no effect on the calculated ES, since no correlation is needed for the calculation. This is a bit strange since the correlation imputation was stated several times in the MA. But I could perfectly recalculate two out of three ES with CI with my method (accuracy of <0.001), and the third was only off by 0.01. Incorrect reference numbers in forest plot
